glossary.definition: Methods used to measure the similarity or distance between clusters to decide the rules for merging them.
glossary.sentence: There are several methods to measure the similarity (distance) between clusters to decide the rules for merging clusters, and they are often called linkage methods .

glossary.definition: A linkage method where cluster similarity is determined by calculating the distance between the clusters’ most distant elements.
glossary.sentence: Complete-linkage : in deciding about the cluster similarity, the distance between the clusters’ most distant elements (the longest distance) is calculated

glossary.definition: A linkage method where cluster similarity is determined by calculating the distance between the closest elements of two clusters.
glossary.sentence: Single-linkage : in deciding about the cluster similarity, the distance between the closest elements of the two clusters (the shortest distance) is calculated

glossary.definition: A linkage method where the distance between two clusters is defined as the average distance between each data object in one cluster to every data object in the other cluster.
glossary.sentence: Average-linkage: the distance between two clusters is defined as the average distance between each data object in one cluster to every data object in the other cluster

glossary.definition: A tree-like diagram that provides a graphical description of hierarchical clustering and represents hierarchical relationships between data objects.
glossary.sentence: A dendrogram is an output of hierarchical clustering. It is a tree-like diagram representing hierarchical relationships between data objects in the dataset.

glossary.definition: Components of a dendrogram that represent the merging of data objects into clusters, with each having a specific height indicating similarity.
glossary.sentence: A dendrogram consists of (Figure 5): clades that represent merging of data objects into clusters

glossary.definition: The terminal points of clades in a dendrogram that correspond to the data objects used in the clustering process.
glossary.sentence: leaves that are terminal of clades corresponding to the data objects used in the clustering process.

glossary.definition: A line drawn through a dendrogram to determine the final number of clusters based on where it intersects vertical lines.
glossary.sentence: A horizontal cut- off line is usually made through the dendrogram to decide the number of clusters based on the hierarchical clustering algorithm.

glossary.definition: Metrics used to compare the performance of different algorithms on the same dataset and/or analyse the behaviour of the same algorithm by tuning values of its hyperparameters.
glossary.sentence: Metrics for evaluating the machine learning model performance are used to: compare the performance of the different algorithms used on the same dataset and/or analyse the behaviour of the same algorithm by tuning values of its hyperparameters.

glossary.definition: A metric that measures the average of the squared prediction errors for individual data objects.
glossary.sentence: The Mean squared error (MSE) measures the average of the squared prediction errors for individual data objects.

glossary.definition: A metric that applies a square root to MSE and provides an error measure in the same unit as the target variable.
glossary.sentence: Root mean square error (RMSE) applies a square root to MSE and therefore provides an error measure in the same unit as the target variable, for example, if the classifier predicts the number of graduates, then the error will represent “the number of persons”; if it predicts the prices of the house in EUR, then the error value will represent “price in EUR”.

glossary.definition: The average of the sums of the absolute difference between predicted output values and actual output values.
glossary.sentence: Mean absolute error (MAE) is the average of the sums of the absolute difference between predicted output values and actual output values.

glossary.definition: Also known as the coefficient of determination, it indicates how close the model built fits the actual data objects available in the dataset.
glossary.sentence: R2 coefficient is also known as the coefficient of determination. It indicates how close the model built (the line/plane found by the regression algorithm) fits the actual data objects available in the dataset.

glossary.definition: Approaches in multiclass classification where the class under consideration is treated as positive while other classes are treated as negative.
glossary.sentence: The typical approach in multiclass classification task is to consider each class as a binary case based on One-vs-Rest approaches in which the class under consideration is treated to be a positive one while other negative ones.

glossary.definition: An outcome where a data object is positive (label 1) and the algorithm correctly recognises it as such.
glossary.sentence: True positives (TP) – a data object is positive (label 1), and the algorithm correctly recognises it as such (label 1).

glossary.definition: An outcome where a data object is negative (label 0) and the algorithm correctly recognises it as such.
glossary.sentence: True negatives (TN) - a data object is negative (label 0), and the algorithm correctly recognises it as such (label 0).

glossary.definition: An erroneous prediction where a data object is negative (label 0) but the algorithm labels it as positive (label 1).
glossary.sentence: False positives (FP) – a data object is negative (label 0), but the algorithm labels it as positive (label 1); for example, an e-mail is classified as spam, but it is not.

glossary.definition: An erroneous prediction where a data object is positive (label 1) but the classifier labels it as negative (label 0).
glossary.sentence: False negatives (FN) – a data object is positive (label 1), but the classifier labels it as negative (label 0), for example, an e-mail is classified as non-spam, but it is spam.

glossary.definition: A two-dimensional table of counts showing how often each class/category is classified or misclassified as each other category.
glossary.sentence: The mentioned outcomes are typically summarised in a confusion matrix (Kubat, 2017) . It is a two-dimensional table of counts of how often each class/category is classified or misclassified as each other category (Russell & Norvig. 2010) .

glossary.definition: A metric that evaluates a fraction of correct predictions over a given set of data objects.
glossary.sentence: Accuracy metric evaluates a fraction of correct predictions over a given set of data objects.

glossary.definition: A metric that evaluates a fraction of predicted positives that are true positives, representing the accuracy of the positive predictions.
glossary.sentence: A precision metric evaluates a fraction of predicted positives that are true positives. In other words, the accuracy of the positive predictions (Geron, 2017) .

glossary.definition: Also called sensitivity and true positive rate, it evaluates a fraction of data objects classified as positives out of the total actual positive data objects.
glossary.sentence: A recall metric (also called sensitivity and true positive rate) evaluates a fraction of data objects classified as positives out of the total actual positive data objects.

glossary.definition: The sum of recall values for all classes divided by the number of classes in the dataset.
glossary.sentence: After that, we calculate the macro average recall for the task by summing up recall values for all classes and dividing the sum by the number of classes in the dataset.

glossary.definition: The relationship in classification where increasing precision reduces recall and vice versa.
glossary.sentence: Unfortunately, you can't have it both ways: increasing precision reduces recall and vice versa. This is called the precision/recall tradeoff .

glossary.definition: The harmonic mean of precision and recall that estimates the number of data objects classified correctly, with values ranging from 0 to 1.
glossary.sentence: It represents the harmonic mean of precision and recall because the classifier will only get a high F1 score if both recall and precision are high (Geron, 2017) .

glossary.definition: A metric in multiclass classification calculated by the macro average precision and recall.
glossary.sentence: In the multiclass classification task, macro F1 can be calculated by the macro average precision and recall.

glossary.definition: A visualization that plots the false positive rate (x-axis) versus the true positive rate (y-axis) for each value of a hyperparameter.
glossary.sentence: The ROC curve plots false positive rate (x-axis) versus true positive rate (y-axis) for each value of the hyperparameter, helping you visualise values that would be good choices for the tradeoff (Russell & Norvig, 2010; Geron, 2017; Kubat, 2017) .

glossary.definition: A single-number summary that measures the entire two-dimensional area underneath the ROC curve, returning a value in the range [0, 1].
glossary.sentence: Area under the ROC Curve (AUC) allows comparing different classifiers. It provides a single-number summary of the ROC curve (Russell & Norvig, 2010) .

glossary.definition: A task of unsupervised machine learning that involves dividing data objects into representative groups.
glossary.sentence: In general, the clustering task demands dividing data objects into representative groups.

glossary.definition: A metric for clustering performance that represents the closeness of each data object in a cluster to data objects in the neighbouring clusters, calculated using average intra-cluster and inter-cluster distances.
glossary.sentence: Therefore, it represents the closeness of each data object in a cluster to data objects in the neighbouring clusters.

glossary.definition: A plot representing the cluster label on the y-axis and the Silhouette Score on the x-axis, where the size of silhouettes is proportional to the number of data objects in a cluster.
glossary.sentence: The Silhouette Scores are usually plotted on the so-called Silhouette Plot , representing the cluster label on the y-axis and the Silhouette Score on the x-axis (Fig.6).

glossary.definition: A cell in the human brain consisting of a soma (cell body) containing a nucleus, dendrites, and a single axon.
glossary.sentence: Each neuron has a cell body (a soma) containing a nucleus and two types of root-like extensions from the cell body: many dendrites and a single axon (Fig.

glossary.definition: Root-like extensions from a neuron's cell body that serve for receiving signals from other neurons.
glossary.sentence: Dendrites look like a little brush and serve for receiving signals from other neurons.

glossary.definition: A single root-like extension from a neuron's cell body that splits into branches and transmits signals to a synapse.
glossary.sentence: The axon itself eventually splits into a number of branches, each terminating in a little endbulb that comes close to the dendrites of another neuron.

glossary.definition: The site where the end of an axon branch comes close to a dendrite or the cell body of another neuron.
glossary.sentence: A sending neuron transmits a signal along its axon to a synapse, which is the site where the end of an axon branch comes close to a dendrite or the cell body of another neuron.

glossary.definition: Synapses that promote the firing of a neuron, conceptually represented as having a positive weight.
glossary.sentence: Some of the signals reaching the neuron's dendrites promote firing, and others inhibit it. These are called excitatory and inhibitory synapses, respectively.

glossary.definition: Synapses that inhibit the firing of a neuron, conceptually represented as having a negative weight.
glossary.sentence: Some of the signals reaching the neuron's dendrites promote firing, and others inhibit it. These are called excitatory and inhibitory synapses, respectively.

glossary.definition: The level of total input strength that must be exceeded for a neuron to fire an electrical impulse.
glossary.sentence: If this total input exceeds the threshold of the neuron, then the neuron will fire (the fragment is adopted from (Bermúdez, 2014)).

glossary.definition: A mathematical model of the neuron defined in 1943 by McCulloch and Pitts that laid the fundamentals for artificial neural networks, typically consisting of inputs, weights, an output, a combining function, and an activation function.
glossary.sentence: In 1943, McCulloch and Pitts defined a mathematical model of the neuron (Ertel, 2011; Jones, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011), which laid fundamentals for artificial neural networks.

glossary.definition: Numerical values that enter from the environment or are delivered by other neurons, typically corresponding to the values of data object features in a dataset.
glossary.sentence: In general, an artificial neuron has (Callan, 2003; Hopgood, 2001; Jones, 2009; Luger, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011): one or more inputs (sometimes also called input signals) which are represented by numerical values that enter from the environment or are delivered by other neurons.

glossary.definition: A numerical value representing either a final solution of the artificial neural network or an output to other neurons.
glossary.sentence: an output that is represented by a numerical value and can be either a final solution of the artificial neural network or output to other neurons.

glossary.definition: A function for combining input signals that sums up the weighted values of inputs to provide a single numerical value.
glossary.sentence: It is typically a summation function that sums up the weighted values of inputs.

glossary.definition: A function that determines the output of the neuron and allows the neural network to model complex non-linear relationships.
glossary.sentence: The transfer (activation) function determines the output of the neuron.

glossary.definition: An input commonly set to one that provides the offset of the line or hyperplane from the origin.
glossary.sentence: a bias input which is also commonly applied, and it provides the offset of the line/hyperplane from the origin.

glossary.definition: Activation functions, specifically the step and sign functions, often used in decision-making neurons for classification and pattern recognition tasks.
glossary.sentence: The step and sign activation functions, also called hard limit functions, are often used in decision-making neurons for classification and pattern recognition tasks ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: A structure comprising one or more artificial neurons connected in some pattern, arranged in a hierarchy of layers including input, output, and hidden layers.
glossary.sentence: An artificial neural network comprises one or more artificial neurons connected in some pattern (Callan, 2003; Jones, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: Single neurons within an artificial neural network that are connected by transmitting signals to each other.
glossary.sentence: Single neurons are called processing units or nodes.

glossary.definition: A layer that does not contain computing neurons and thus does not make processing; it accepts input signals from the environment and sends these signals to hidden layers.
glossary.sentence: an input layer that accepts input signals from the environment and sends these signals to hidden layers. It does not include computing neurons;

glossary.definition: A layer containing computing neurons that provides the final output of the network and sends the numerical output to the environment.
glossary.sentence: an output layer that sends the numerical output produced by the neural network to the environment and consists of computing neurons;

glossary.definition: Layers in a neural network where the error for each processing unit is calculated specifically to update the weights of hidden neurons.
glossary.sentence: In the hidden layers, the error for each processing unit is calculated in the following way:

glossary.definition: A characteristic of artificial neural networks where all neurons in a layer process their inputs simultaneously and independently.
glossary.sentence: Moreover, artificial neural networks have a parallel processing nature because all the neurons in a layer process their inputs simultaneously and independently (Jones, 2009; Luger, 2009).

glossary.definition: A type of artificial neural network with connections only in one direction, where every processing unit receives input from upstream units and delivers output to downstream units without loops.
glossary.sentence: A feed-forward network has connections only in one dir • ection . Every processing unit receives input from "upstream" processing units and delivers output to "downstream" processing units; there are no loops.

glossary.definition: A type of artificial neural network that feeds its outputs back into its inputs, forming a dynamic system that may reach a stable state or exhibit oscillations.
glossary.sentence: A recurrent network , on the other hand, feeds its outputs back into its inputs.

glossary.definition: The process of modifying weights in some particular way, such as applying a learning rule to adapt to differences between desired and actual output.
glossary.sentence: Learning in artificial neural networks is related to modifying weights in some particular way .

glossary.definition: The resistance of a network to partially or entirely damaged processing units or connections, where operation changes very little because knowledge is distributed across the network.
glossary.sentence: It is called fault tolerance, or, in other words, graceful degradation, and it is explained by the fact that the knowledge of the network is not stored in one place, but it is distributed all over the network ( Borisovs et al., 1998).

glossary.definition: The selection of the number of layers, the number of processing units, and the connectivity pattern for an artificial neural network.
glossary.sentence: to select the architecture of the network: the number of layers, the number of processing units and the connectivity pattern;

glossary.definition: A case of machine learning where the final output of the network can be compared with the desired (actual label) output to determine if a learning rule should be applied.
glossary.sentence: It means that after processing the input signals in the whole network, the artificial neural network receives a final output which in the case of supervised machine learning can be compared with the desired (actual label) output.

glossary.definition: Identifying the structure or pattern in data.
glossary.sentence: pattern recognition which assumes identifying the structure or pattern in data;

glossary.definition: The process of projecting probable consequences of given situations.
glossary.sentence: Prediction - projecting probable consequences of given situations.

glossary.definition: Finding the "best" organization of constraints.
glossary.sentence: optimization or, in other words, finding the "best" organization of constraints;

glossary.definition: Separating signal from background, factoring out the irrelevant components of a signal.
glossary.sentence: noise filtering, or separating signal from background, factoring out the irrelevant components of a signal;

glossary.definition: Determining the values of variables that cannot be measured easily but which are known to depend in some complex way on other more accessible variables.
glossary.sentence: non-linear estimation determining the values of variables that cannot be measured easily but which are known to depend in some complex way on other more accessible variables and others.

glossary.definition: A model developed by Rosenblatt in the late 1950s.
glossary.sentence: Rosenblatt developed the perceptron model in the late 1950s (Jones, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: The simplest and the commonly known type of artificial neural network which can learn only linearly separable functions and has only one processing layer where all inputs are connected directly to the outputs.
glossary.sentence: A Single Layer Perceptron is the simplest and the commonly known type of artificial neural network, but its practical applicability is limited because it can learn only linearly separable functions.

glossary.definition: A rule applied to compute weight correction for adjustments of the weights of the perceptron.
glossary.sentence: The weight correction is usually computed by applying the delta rule :

glossary.definition: One cycle in which the artificial neural network uses all of the data objects contained in the training dataset exactly once for learning purposes.
glossary.sentence: An epoch represents one cycle in which the artificial neural network uses all of the data objects contained in the training dataset exactly once for learning purposes.

glossary.definition: The sum of squared differences between the actual and predicted outputs for all output neurons for all training examples.
glossary.sentence: The total error of the network is calculated as the sum of squared differences between the actual and predicted outputs for all output neurons for all training examples.

glossary.definition: A basic type of feed-forward neural network that, with one hidden layer, can represent any continuous function of the input signals and with two hidden layers - even discontinuous functions.
glossary.sentence: The multilayer perceptron is a basic type of feed-forward neural network that, with one hidden layer, can represent any continuous function of the input signals and with two hidden layers - even discontinuous functions ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: A network that has greater processing power and can model practically any function of arbitrary complexity, where the number of inputs and the number of hidden layers determine the function complexity.
glossary.sentence: Therefore, a feed-forward neural network has greater processing power, and it can model practically any function of arbitrary complexity, where the number of inputs and the number of hidden layers determine the function complexity (Jones, 2009).

glossary.definition: A layer that consists of computing neurons and usually performs the detection of features.
glossary.sentence: at least one hidden layer that consists of computing neurons and usually performs the detection of features.

glossary.definition: An algorithm that allows modifying weights in all network layers through the propagation of the error from the output layer to the input layer.
glossary.sentence: The backpropagation algorithm allows modifying weights in all network layers through the propagation of the error from the output layer to the input layer (Fig.

glossary.definition: The propagation of inputs forward in the network from the input layer to the output layer.
glossary.sentence: The inputs are propagated over the network from the input layer to the output layer during the feed-forwarding .

glossary.definition: The propagation of error backwards in the network: from the output layer to the previous hidden layer, and from this hidden layer back to a hidden layer before, until reaching the weights of the input layer.
glossary.sentence: performing backpropagation of error backwards in the network: from the output layer to the previous hidden layer, and from this hidden layer back to a hidden layer before, etc. until reaching the weights of the input layer;

glossary.definition: Neurons for which the predicted output and the real class label are known, enabling the calculation of error to update weights.
glossary.sentence: In the output neuron, we know the predicted output and the real class label and, thus, can calculate the error in the following way:

glossary.definition: A storage mechanism for knowledge that has an unlimited capacity and provides long-term storage.
glossary.sentence: This memory has an unlimited capacity and provides a long-term storage of knowledge.

glossary.definition: An intelligent computer system composed of two main components: a knowledge base and an inference engine.
glossary.sentence: A knowledge-based intelligent system is an intelligent computer system that contains two main components – the knowledge base and the inference engine (Hopgood, 2001).

glossary.definition: A storage component for real-world problems that includes a considerable number of production rules and where knowledge is kept separate from the processing mechanism.
glossary.sentence: The knowledge base for real-world problems includes a considerable number of production rules.

glossary.definition: A component of an expert system whose task is to relate production rules to find a logical reasoning chain consisting of known facts and conclusions that they support.
glossary.sentence: Therefore, a task of the inference engine is to relate production rules to find a logical reasoning chain consisting of known facts and conclusions that they support (Mallach, 1994).

glossary.definition: A storage system for unrelated facts, such as purchase transactions or student grades.
glossary.sentence: The database stores unrelated facts, for example, purchase transactions or grades of students.

glossary.definition: A specialist proficient in artificial intelligence methods and tools who understands how to describe and represent knowledge in a knowledge base.
glossary.sentence: The knowledge engineer is a specialist who knows well methods and tools of artificial intelligence, as well as who has knowledge how to describe and represent knowledge in the knowledge base.

glossary.definition: An individual with deep knowledge of facts and rules and substantial practical experience in a particular, usually limited, domain.
glossary.sentence: Anyone can be considered a domain expert if he/she has deep knowledge (of both facts and rules) and substantial practical experience in a particular (usually limited) domain ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: An automated system used by domain experts or knowledge engineers to describe knowledge and construct a knowledge base, often providing user interfaces and consistency checking tools.
glossary.sentence: The knowledge acquisition system is an automated system that could be used either by the domain expert directly or by the knowledge engineer to describe knowledge and to build a knowledge base.

glossary.definition: Knowledge-based intelligent systems that emulate a human expert's methodology and performance to solve problems in narrowly specialized domains.
glossary.sentence: Intelligent computer systems that emulate the human expert’s methodology and performance are called expert systems (Luger, 2009).

glossary.definition: The process of forming high-level conclusions from collections of raw data.
glossary.sentence: Interpretation - forming high-level conclusions from collections of raw data.

glossary.definition: determining the cause of malfunctions in complex situations based on observable symptoms.
glossary.sentence: Diagnosis - determining the cause of malfunctions in complex situations based on observable symptoms.

glossary.definition: finding a configuration of system components that meets performance goals while satisfying a set of design constraints.
glossary.sentence: Design - finding a configuration of system components that meets performance goals while satisfying a set of design constraints.

glossary.definition: devising a sequence of actions that will achieve a set of goals given certain starting conditions and run-time constraints.
glossary.sentence: Planning - devising a sequence of actions that will achieve a set of goals given certain starting conditions and run-time constraints.

glossary.definition: comparing a system’s observed behaviour to its expected behaviour.
glossary.sentence: Monitoring - comparing a system’s observed behaviour to its expected behaviour (for example, instruction-assisting in the education process in technical domains).

glossary.definition: governing the behaviour of a complex environment.
glossary.sentence: Control - governing the behaviour of a complex environment.

glossary.definition: means that offer a set of formal conventions and rules used to describe knowledge.
glossary.sentence: Knowledge representation schemas are means that offer a set of formal conventions and rules used to describe knowledge.

glossary.definition: represent knowledge in the form of logical statements, using constants, variables, functions, predicates, logical operators, and apply inference rules that allow getting new true knowledge from a set of true knowledge.
glossary.sentence: logical knowledge representation schemas represent knowledge in the form of logical statements, using constants, variables, functions, predicates, logical operators, and apply inference rules that allow getting new true knowledge from a set of true knowledge;

glossary.definition: represent knowledge in the form of a directed labelled graph, where each node displays a simple object/concept and directed labelled arcs represent relations between them.
glossary.sentence: networked knowledge representation schemas represent knowledge in the form of a directed labelled graph, where each node displays a simple object/concept and directed labelled arcs represent relations between them;

glossary.definition: Schemas that use a graph to represent knowledge where each node represents a complex structure describing an object/concept together with its properties and parts.
glossary.sentence: Structured knowledge representation schemas also use a graph to represent knowledge.

glossary.term: Procedural or rule-based knowledge representation schemas
glossary.definition: represent knowledge in the form of a set of procedures/instructions.
glossary.sentence: procedural or rule-based knowledge representation schemas represent knowledge in the form of a set of procedures/instructions.

glossary.definition: theories that postulate that knowledge on different concepts is related by associations in the human mind and these associations define the meaning of a concept in relation to meaning of other concepts.
glossary.sentence: These theories postulate that knowledge on different concepts is related by associations in the human mind and these associations define the meaning of a concept in relation to meaning of other concepts (Luger, 2009), for example, the concept “university” for the most part of students could be associated with such concepts as lectures, students, professors, exams, science etc.

glossary.definition: a knowledge representation schema that represents knowledge as a graph: labelled nodes in this graph correspond to domain objects/concepts but directed labelled arcs – to relations between them.
glossary.sentence: A semantic network is a knowledge representation schema that represents knowledge as a graph: labelled nodes in this graph correspond to domain objects/concepts but directed labelled arcs – to relations between them (Callan, 2003; Luger, 2009; Mallach, 1994; Winston, 1992).

glossary.definition: represented by a title of “is a” (also “is a subclass of”, “is a subset of”, “is a type of” or “is a kind of”) and allows showing hierarchical relations by relating narrower concepts to broader concepts.
glossary.sentence: A relation “subclass-class” (also “subset-set”, “subtype-type”, “ subkind -kind”) is represented by a title of “is a” (also “is a subclass of”, “is a subset of”, “is a type of” or “is a kind of”).

glossary.definition: includes a set of concepts/objects that share common properties.
glossary.sentence: A class/set/type/kind includes a set of concepts/objects that share common properties.

glossary.definition: represented by the title of “is an instance/example of” and allows creating hierarchical relations by relating one specific object in the problem domain to a class/set/type/kind it belongs to.
glossary.sentence: A relation “instance-class/set/type/kind” is represented by the title of “is an instance/example of”.

glossary.definition: represented by the title of “Part of” and allows relating parts of the object to the whole object.
glossary.sentence: A relation “part-whole” is represented by the title of “Part of” (Fig.8).

glossary.definition: represented by the title of “property/has a property of” and allows relating objects/concepts to their properties.
glossary.sentence: A relation “object-property” is represented by the title of “property/has a property of” (Fig.9).

glossary.definition: a characteristic of an object/concept that can be measured by quantitative (numerical) or qualitative (descriptive) values.
glossary.sentence: A property is a characteristic of an object/concept that can be measured by quantitative (numerical) or qualitative (descriptive) values.

glossary.definition: represented by the title of “value” and allows relating properties of objects/concepts to their values.
glossary.sentence: A relation “property-value” is represented by the title of “value” (Fig.10).

glossary.definition: relations that allow specifying any other kind of relations (for example, like, own, travel, etc.) that cannot be represented by the standard types of relations.
glossary.sentence: They allow specifying any other kind of relations (for example, like, own, travel, etc.) that cannot be represented by the previously mentioned types of relations.

glossary.definition: An essential feature where sub-class and instance frames inherit slots and their values from class frames, supporting overwriting of inherited values.
glossary.sentence: Inheritance is an essential feature of frame-based expert systems ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: knowledge about facts, objects, and concepts.
glossary.sentence: they represent only declarative knowledge (knowledge about facts, objects, and concepts );

glossary.definition: A principle used when one node references another node by its name, such as when a unique title of one frame is used as a slot value in another frame.
glossary.sentence: Moreover, relations are not represented explicitly by arcs at it was done in semantic networks, but a referencing principle is used when one node references another node by its name.

glossary.definition: A data structure for representing a stereotyped situation, typical knowledge about an object/concept, or information relevant to stereotyped entities.
glossary.sentence: A frame is “a data-structure for representing a stereotyped situation” (Minsky, 1975).

glossary.definition: Collections of related frames linked together.
glossary.sentence: According to Minsky (1975), “collections of related frames are linked together into frame-systems” .

glossary.definition: Properties of objects/concepts that can have symbolic, numerical, or boolean values, be empty, or contain a title of another frame.
glossary.sentence: Slots are properties of objects/concepts (Jones, 2009; Mallach, 1994; Negnevitsky , 2011; Winston, 1992).

glossary.definition: An extension to the slot-value structure of frames that represent extended knowledge about slot values and tell the inference engine how to process the property of an object.
glossary.sentence: Facets are an extension to the slot-value structure of frames as they represent extended knowledge about slot values and tell the inference engine how to process the property of the object, allow to set a slot value and control user's access to the slot value ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: A frame containing common properties of a set, class, kind, or type of objects/concepts, helping to represent a problem in an abstract form.
glossary.sentence: The class frames help us to represent a problem in an abstract form ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: Frames that inherit slots from a high-level class frame while also containing new, specific slots.
glossary.sentence: Next, there are two sub-class frames: Cat and Dog . They inherit slots from the Animal class frame, and each of them has new slots added.

glossary.definition: A frame representing detailed knowledge about a particular object in the problem domain, typically derived from class or sub-class frames and including all slot values.
glossary.sentence: It represents a detailed knowledge about a particular object in the problem domain (Figure 6).

glossary.definition: A relation defined between instances and classes, or sub-classes and classes, represented as a Class slot.
glossary.sentence: The generalization relation is defined between TreeAlex-Person , BrownLucie-Person , Tom-Cat , Rex-Dog , Cat-Animal , Dog-Animal . It is represented as a Class slot in the sub-class frames and instance frames.

glossary.definition: A relation representing extended knowledge about parts of objects where frames for components are associated with a frame representing the whole object.
glossary.sentence: Aggregation relation . It allows representing extended knowledge about parts of objects/concepts (Figure 9).

glossary.definition: A relation that allows defining any other type of relations by using the referencing principle.
glossary.sentence: Association or semantic relation . It allows defining any other type of relations by using the referencing principle (Figure 10).

glossary.definition: A feature where a frame inherits slots and slot values from several other frames simultaneously, provided the parent slot titles are unique.
glossary.sentence: Multiple inheritance when a frame inherits slots and slot values from several other frames simultaneously is allowed if slot titles of parent frames are unique.

glossary.definition: The addition of regular procedures and/or demons to a slot within a frame.
glossary.sentence: A slot can have a procedural attachment ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: A self-contained piece of computer code written by a developer for specific purposes like generating images, calculating values, or acquiring data.
glossary.sentence: A regular procedure is a self-contained piece of computer code written by the developer of an intelligent computer system ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: A procedure invoked as a side effect of another action in the knowledge base, triggered automatically when a slot value is accessed.
glossary.sentence: A demon is a procedure that is invoked as a side effect of some other action in the knowledge base (Luger, 2009).

glossary.definition: Frames that have demons assigned to them.
glossary.sentence: Object-oriented frames are acquired when demons are assigned to frames.

glossary.definition: A standard demon triggered when a slot value is unspecified and must be calculated or set.
glossary.sentence: IF-NEEDED – it is triggered when the slot value is unspecified, and it is necessary to calculate/to set it;

glossary.definition: A standard demon triggered when a slot value changes.
glossary.sentence: IF-CHANGED – it is triggered when a slot value changes;

glossary.definition: A standard demon triggered when a slot value is unspecified and is added to the frame.
glossary.sentence: IF-ADDED – it is triggered when a slot value is unspecified, and it is added to the frame;

glossary.definition: A standard demon triggered when a slot value is deleted.
glossary.sentence: IF-REMOVED – it is triggered when a slot value is deleted.

glossary.definition: A structured way to store knowledge about objects in one place that provides hierarchical organization, supports inheritance, and allows for the representation of various object knowledge including executable demons.
glossary.sentence: Frames have the following advantages: frames store knowledge about each object in one place in a structured way; frames provide the hierarchical organization of knowledge; frames allow the representation of various knowledge about objects; frames support inheritance; frames allow adding executable demons to slots to control access to slot values and, therefore, ensure the knowledge base's integrity.

glossary.definition: An executable demon that calculates specific values, such as discounts, when the information is requested or when triggered by the inference engine during a search.
glossary.sentence: The demon IF-NEEDED will calculate the amount of discount for a particular client when the information on the discount will be requested.

glossary.definition: A mechanism that searches for specified properties of an object by checking slots, performing inheritance, triggering demons, and exploring local relations until a value is obtained.
glossary.sentence: The inference engine of frames is based on the following principles: to acquire knowledge about some object/concept, find a corresponding frame and check its slots.

glossary.definition: Top-level frames in a frame-based knowledge base that represent broad categories, such as Person and Animal.
glossary.sentence: First, there are two high-level class frames: Person and Animal .

glossary.definition: Frames that represent specific individual examples of a particular class frame.
glossary.sentence: Finally, there are four instance frames: TreeAlex and BrownLucie are instances for the Person class frame; Tom is an instance for the Cat class frame; Rex is an instance for the Dog class frame.

glossary.definition: Relations between frames that represent connections such as ownership or emotional reactions.
glossary.sentence: The frame system also represents the following associative relations between frames: BrownLucie - Tom , the relation Owns , and Tom- BrownLucie , the relation Animal owner; TreeAlex - Rex , the relation Owns , and Rex- TreeAlex , the relation Animal owner; Tom – Rex , the relation Afraid of .

glossary.definition: A knowledge-based intelligent system in which domain knowledge is represented as a set of rules.
glossary.sentence: A rule-based expert system is a knowledge-based intelligent system in which domain knowledge is represented in the form of a set of rules (Hopgood, 2001).

glossary.definition: Schemas used to develop rule-based systems that represent problem-solving instructions rather than knowledge about objects or concepts.
glossary.sentence: Rule-based or procedural knowledge representation schemas are used to develop such systems. They represent a set of problem-solving instructions not a knowledge about objects/concepts.

glossary.definition: A chunk of knowledge about solving a problem presented in an IF-THEN format consisting of a condition and a consequence.
glossary.sentence: Each production rule is a chunk of knowledge about solving a problem (Luger, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: Rule-based expert systems consisting of a knowledge base of rules, a working memory, and an inference engine.
glossary.sentence: Production systems are rule-based expert systems that consist of three parts (Callan, 2003; Luger, 2005; Negnevitsky , 2011): a knowledge base that contains a set of production rules; a working memory; an inference engine.

glossary.definition: A component of a production system containing a set of production rules often interrelated by sharing knowledge units between their IF and THEN parts.
glossary.sentence: The knowledge base typically includes many production rules interrelated by sharing knowledge: knowledge units in the THEN part of one rule make the IF part of another rule (Mallach, 1994).

glossary.definition: A storage area where problem descriptions, patterns, goals, and the results of firing production rules are added during the search process.
glossary.sentence: the search begins with a problem description or given data about the problem (a pattern) that are added to the working memory;

glossary.definition: A description of the current state of the problem-solving process stored within the working memory.
glossary.sentence: The working memory contains a description of the current state of the problem-solving process (Callan, 2003; Luger, 2009) that is called a pattern .

glossary.definition: A component that carries out reasoning by interpreting and applying rules to facts in the working memory using a Recognize-act control cycle.
glossary.sentence: The inference engine performs the control cycle "Recognize-act" (Luger, 2009) to link the rules given in the knowledge base with the facts provided in the working memory (Negnevitsky, 2011).

glossary.definition: Components that allow an expert system to justify its output and explain the line of reasoning used to reach a conclusion.
glossary.sentence: Explanation facilities are essential for any expert system because an expert system provides the user with a recommendation, plan, advice, conclusion, or any other result.

glossary.definition: The mechanism through which a user interacts with the expert system, which can be graphical, natural language, menu-driven, or question-and-answer based.
glossary.sentence: The user interacts with the system through a user interface designed as a question-and-answer, menu-driven, natural language, or graphical interface (Luger, 2009).

glossary.definition: An interface intended for editing rules in the knowledge base and checking system performance after updates.
glossary.sentence: the developer interface is intended to edit rules in the knowledge base (add new rules as well as modify and delete existing rules) and to check the performance of the expert system after updating the rules ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: A process that matches a pattern against the IF or THEN parts of production rules in the knowledge base to determine which rules to fire.
glossary.sentence: The control cycle "Recognize-act" matches the pattern against the IF part of production rules in the knowledge base;

glossary.definition: The set of enabled production rules that are eligible to be fired during an iteration.
glossary.sentence: Conflict resolution is the method of choosing one rule to fire from those that are able to fire, i.e., from the set of enabled rules, known as the conflict set (Hopgood, 2001; Luger, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: The method of choosing one rule to fire from a set of enabled rules that are capable of firing.
glossary.sentence: Conflict resolution is the method of choosing one rule to fire from those that are able to fire, i.e., from the set of enabled rules, known as the conflict set (Hopgood, 2001; Luger, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: The action of triggering an enabled rule, which subsequently modifies the content of the working memory.
glossary.sentence: one of the enabled rules is fired, and it changes the content of the working memory because its IF or THEN part is added to the working memory (depending on the search direction );

glossary.definition: Independent units of knowledge that offer a unified way of storing information, typically using "if... then..." constructions to represent problem-solving logic.
glossary.sentence: Production rules offer a unified way of storing knowledge because each rule is an independent unit of knowledge; thus, the very syntax of production rules enables them to be self-documented ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: A conflict resolution method based on the assumption that a specific rule processes more information than a general one, preferring rules with more conditions.
glossary.sentence: This method is also known as the longest matching strategy: it is based on the assumption that a specific rule processes more information than a general one ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: Markers attached to each fact in the working memory that allow the expert system to identify and fire rules using the most recently added data.
glossary.sentence: This method relies on time tags attached to each fact in the working memory; thus, in the conflict set, the expert system first fires the rule whose antecedent uses the data most recently added to the working memory (Negnevitsky, 2011).

glossary.definition: A property where rules have no syntactic interactions, meaning they cannot call each other directly or set variables in other rules, allowing for incremental system development.
glossary.sentence: The modularity of rules and the iterative nature of their execution make it easier to trace the execution of production rules.

glossary.definition: An architectural advantage where knowledge is stored in the knowledge base and the processing mechanism is the inference engine, allowing either to be modified independently.
glossary.sentence: Knowledge is stored in the knowledge base, but its processing mechanism is the inference engine.

glossary.definition: A characteristic of production rules where each rule acts as an independent unit of knowledge, enabling them to be self-documented.
glossary.sentence: Production rules offer a unified way of storing knowledge because each rule is an independent unit of knowledge; thus, the very syntax of production rules enables them to be self-documented ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: Methods used to make the search process more effective, including selection of search direction, rule sequence and structure, and conflict resolution strategy.
glossary.sentence: Three search control strategies are used for this purpose (Luger, 2009): control through the selection of search direction; control through the selection of rule sequence and structure; control through the selection of conflict resolution strategy.
